title: Liquid Castile soap
subtitle: A 100% olive oil liquid soap made with potassium hydroxide and diluted for everyday use
excerpt: True liquid Castile soap is 100% olive oil saponified with potassium hydroxide (KOH) rather than sodium hydroxide. The result is a soft paste that dilutes with distilled water into a gentle, golden liquid soap suitable for hands, body, and household use.
sourceNotes: Liquid Castile soap formulation based on Kevin Dunn's Scientific Soapmaking and Soap Queen liquid soap guides. KOH purity adjustment calculations standard industry practice.

recipe.yieldDescription: Approx 750 ml finished liquid soap (from 500 g oil, diluted 1:5)
recipe.batchNotes: Scale oil and KOH proportionally. Adjust dilution water to desired consistency.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The soap paste can be stored undiluted for months. Dilute in small batches as needed.
recipe.temperatureNote: Cook the soap paste in a slow cooker on low (around 70 to 80 degrees Celsius).
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 12 months once diluted, stored in a pump or flip-top bottle. The undiluted paste keeps indefinitely in a sealed jar. Discard diluted soap if it clouds, smells off, or separates without shaking.

glossaryTerms.definition: The alkali used to make liquid and soft soap. Unlike sodium hydroxide (NaOH) which makes hard bars, KOH produces a softer salt that dissolves readily in water. Commercial KOH is typically 90% pure, so the quantity must be adjusted upward by roughly 11%.

The concentrated product of hot-process liquid soap making before dilution. It has a texture similar to petroleum jelly or stiff honey. Diluting 1 part paste in 4 to 5 parts distilled water gives a pourable liquid soap.
A soap-making method that applies external heat (usually in a slow cooker or oven) to accelerate and complete saponification during making, rather than relying on residual heat over weeks of curing.

A quick check on liquid soap paste: dissolve a small amount in boiling water. A clear or slightly golden result indicates fully saponified soap. A milky or cloudy result means the cook needs more time.
The chemical reaction between a fat or oil and an alkali that produces soap and glycerol.

content.text: rather than the sodium hydroxide used in bar soap. The
chemistry is the same but the resulting salt is soft and water-soluble. The
method drives the reaction to completion in a slow cooker, producing a concentrated
that you then dilute. Using 100% olive oil qualifies the result as true Castile soap. Shelf life of the diluted soap is 12 months. The undiluted paste keeps indefinitely.
Potassium hydroxide is as caustic as sodium hydroxide. Read and apply the
guidance in full before starting. Wear protective goggles and gloves. Pour KOH into water, not the reverse. The solution will heat sharply.

items.prepNote: Adjusted for 90% purity; confirm with a liquid soap lye calculator
Approximately 1:5 paste to water; adjust to desired consistency

content.text: technique. Pour the olive oil into the slow cooker and set to low. In a heat-resistant jug, add KOH to the distilled water and stir until dissolved. Pour the KOH solution into the olive oil and stick-blend until the mixture emulsifies and reaches a thick, pudding-like trace.
Place the lid on the slow cooker and cook on low for 2 to 3 hours, stirring every 30 minutes. The paste will progress from a waxy, translucent stage to a shiny applesauce texture. Perform the
: dissolve a teaspoon of paste in a cup of boiling water. A clear golden result confirms the cook is complete. A milky result means 30 more minutes of cooking is needed.

To dilute: weigh out the paste and add an equal weight of boiling distilled water. Stir gently until dissolved, then add more water until the consistency is right. A 1:5 paste-to-water ratio gives a thin, pourable soap. Adjust upward for a thicker, gel-like product.

Undiluted paste keeps indefinitely in a sealed jar at room temperature. Diluted soap keeps for 12 months in a pump bottle. Discard if the soap turns cloudy (other than temporary cloudiness in cold conditions), smells sour, or develops mould.
